era_context: Late Bronze Age, mid-18th Dynasty Egypt under Amenhotep III. The New Kingdom is at its height; Egypt controls Nubia and maintains hegemony over the Levant. The Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I dominates Anatolia and northern Syria. Mycenaean Greece is in the Late Bronze Age IIIA period. China is in the Shang Dynasty.

events.title: Amenhotep III's reign; Egyptian prosperity and cultural achievement
events.description: Amenhotep III's reign (c. 1390–1352 BCE) is characterized by unprecedented prosperity, cultural achievement, and artistic sophistication. The pharaoh is engaged in an extensive building programme that includes temples, palaces, and monuments. Egyptian art and literature reach a high point of refinement. The reign is marked by relative peace and stability, allowing for cultural and economic development. Amenhotep III is one of the most celebrated pharaohs of the New Kingdom.
key_figures: Amenhotep, son of Hapu (architect and official)

sources.name: Temple inscriptions and reliefs at Karnak, Luxor, and other sites
Archaeological evidence from temple and palace sites

events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III's reign is well-attested through multiple contemporary sources, including temple inscriptions, reliefs, and archaeological evidence. His reign is well-dated through king lists and astronomical observations.

events.title: Šuppiluliuma I's rise to power; early military campaigns in Anatolia
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I (r. c. 1370–1330 BCE) is in the early stages of his reign, consolidating power and launching military campaigns to expand Hittite control over Anatolia and northern Syria. His military campaigns are characterized by the use of advanced chariot technology and iron weapons. By this period, the Hittite Empire is becoming the dominant military power in the Near East, rivaling Egyptian influence. Šuppiluliuma's reign marks a turning point in Hittite history, establishing the empire as a major power.
events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's reign is well-attested in Hittite archives, though the precise chronology of individual campaigns is debated (±5–10 years).

events.title: Mycenaean Greece in Late Bronze Age IIIA; palace centers at their height
events.description: Mycenaean palace centers (Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos) are at their height, controlling regional trade networks and producing Linear B administrative records. The palaces are engaged in extensive trade with Egypt, the Levant, and Anatolia. Palatial economies are based on centralized control of agricultural production, craft production, and military forces. This period represents the apex of Mycenaean civilization before the Late Bronze Age collapse (c. 1200 BCE).

sources.name: Linear B tablets from Pylos and Knossos
Archaeological evidence from palace sites (Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos)

events.certainty_note: Mycenaean chronology is based on pottery typology and Egyptian synchronisms; absolute dating for individual years is uncertain (±10–20 years). This year is assigned within the Late Bronze Age IIIA period by convention.

events.title: Shang Dynasty in China; oracle bone divination and bronze technology
events.description: The Shang Dynasty (c. 1600–1046 BCE) continues to develop sophisticated bronze-working techniques and oracle bone divination practices. The Shang state is organized around a royal court that controls bronze production, agricultural surplus, and military forces. Oracle bones are inscribed with questions about harvests, warfare, and divine will, then heated and interpreted. This period represents the continuation of Shang cultural and technological practices that define the dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Shang chronology is debated; this year is assigned within the broader Shang period based on archaeological stratigraphy, but specific events are not documented for individual years. Absolute dating for the Shang Dynasty remains uncertain (±50–100 years).

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Amenhotep III as a 'peaceful' pharaoh has been challenged by recent scholarship, which points to military campaigns in Nubia and the Levant. Some scholars argue that Amenhotep III's reign was marked by more military activity than traditionally assumed. The dating of Šuppiluliuma I's reign has been subject to revision; some scholars place his reign slightly earlier or later than the traditional c. 1370–1330 BCE. Mycenaean chronology is highly debated; some scholars propose a 'low chronology' that would place this year significantly later in absolute terms. Shang chronology remains one of the most contested areas in East Asian history; some scholars propose dates that differ by 50–100 years from the traditional chronology.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1370 BCE is dominated by Egyptian and Hittite sources, with Mycenaean evidence from Linear B tablets providing a third perspective. Egyptian temple inscriptions and reliefs are biased toward royal achievement and may not reflect the full complexity of the period. Hittite sources are similarly focused on royal military campaigns. Mycenaean evidence from Linear B tablets is difficult to interpret and remains contested among scholars. The Levantine perspective is almost entirely absent from the historical record, filtered through Egyptian and Hittite sources. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and the Americas remain absent from the documented record. Shang Dynasty sources are limited to oracle bones and later textual traditions; contemporary written records for specific years are non-existent.

graph_edges.note: Amenhotep III and Šuppiluliuma I were contemporaries; their reigns overlapped and their policies toward the Levant and Near East were in competition.
Šuppiluliuma I's military campaigns and consolidation of Hittite power eventually led to the Battle of Kadesh (c. 1274 BCE) between his successors and Ramesses II.
Mycenaean palace economies documented in this period eventually collapsed during the Late Bronze Age collapse (c. 1200 BCE).
